SB1086.title: Zoning: allowed uses: microschools and micro-education entities.
SB1086.author: Megan Dahle
SB1086.summary: An act to add Section 65852.10 to the Government Code, relating to land use.

actions.description: Introduced. Read first time. To Com. on RLS. for assignment. To print.
From printer. May be acted upon on or after March 16.

analysis.notes: SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10, which would require cities and counties to allow microschools and micro-education entities as permitted uses in zoning districts — overriding local land use decisions about where educational uses are appropriate. This constitutes a partial preemption of local zoning authority (Criterion A), likely mandates ministerial or by-right approval for qualifying micro-education uses (Criterion B/C), and shifts permitting and compliance administration costs to local governments without evident state funding (Criterion D).
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10 to require cities and counties to permit microschools and micro-education entities as allowed uses in local zoning, overriding local land use authority to determine where educational uses are appropriate.
• Preempts local zoning authority by mandating that microschools be treated as allowed uses regardless of a city's or county's general plan and zoning code designations.
• Likely eliminates or severely restricts local discretionary review — conditional use permits, design review, and neighborhood compatibility hearings — for qualifying micro-education facilities.
• Forces cities to approve a new category of institutional use that communities have not chosen to permit, effectively mandating development decisions beyond local preference.
• Shifts administrative and compliance costs onto local planning departments without identified state funding to offset the new permitting and enforcement obligations.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill explicitly overrides local zoning authority across all district types with no local opt-out or mitigation mechanism, and strips discretionary review for a new institutional use category cities have not chosen to permit.

analysis.comms_brief:
AB1751 (Missing Middle Townhome Ownership Act) creates a statewide ministerial pathway for townhome development on any residential parcel, overriding local single-family zoning without community input.
• Cities lose all discretionary authority — projects meeting state objective standards must be approved regardless of neighborhood character, infrastructure capacity, or local planning
• Eliminates design review and public hearings for qualifying developments; no CEQA review applies
• No impact fee authority: cities cannot charge water, sewer, traffic, or school impact fees for new townhome residents — infrastructure costs shift entirely to existing taxpayers
• Sets a precedent for further ministerial preemption of residential zoning across the state
Recommended: CSF should actively oppose; submit formal comment letters and coordinate with planning directors to document infrastructure and service capacity concerns.

Compressed statutory deadlines for local action partially limit local deliberative authority (Criterion A/moderate), and mandatory compliance timelines impose unfunded staffing and administrative burdens on city planning departments without state cost offsets (Criterion D/moderate).
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 1997 amends the Permit Streamlining Act and adds new Health & Safety Code requirements to tighten approval timelines and processes for housing developments, reducing cities' practical ability to conduct thorough review within compressed windows.
• Shortened statutory deadlines under §65950 constrain city planning staff capacity and limit the time available for substantive discretionary review, effectively pressuring approvals regardless of local readiness.
• New Health & Safety Code §50411 obligations create additional compliance requirements that cities must absorb without identified state funding, imposing unfunded administrative burdens on local governments.
• Accelerated timelines reduce cities' ability to condition approvals on full infrastructure and capacity reviews, shifting risk of inadequate review onto existing residents.
Recommended: Monitor — risk is significant but hinges on specific deadline language and penalty provisions; seek amendments to include state funding for compliance costs and preserve infrastructure review windows.

Removing or restricting that owner-occupancy condition strips a key local gatekeeping tool, enabling investor-driven lot splits that cities cannot discretionarily deny, and extends ministerial by-right approval to a broader class of applicants. The Civil Code addition (Section 1102.6l) likely imposes a disclosure requirement tied to lot split eligibility, further embedding state oversight into what was previously a local land use determination. Cost burden on cities is indirect — increased ministerial processing load without new fee authority.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2005 amends the SB 9 urban lot split framework to curtail or remove the owner-occupancy requirement that cities currently use to limit investor-driven lot splits, mandating ministerial approval for a wider class of applicants regardless of local preference.
• Weakens or eliminates the owner-occupancy condition in Government Code 66411.7, stripping cities of a primary tool to screen out speculative lot splits
• Extends mandatory ministerial (no-discretion) urban lot split approvals to non-owner-occupant applicants, overriding local land use judgment
• Adds a state-mandated Civil Code disclosure requirement, further embedding state authority into a process cities previously controlled
• Increased ministerial processing volume with no new local fee authority to offset administrative costs
Recommended: Oppose — removes the one meaningful local safeguard in the SB 9 lot split process with no replacement mechanism for cities to manage speculative development pressure.

AB2288.title: Adaptive reuse: industrial zones.
AB2288.author: Sharon Quirk-Silva
AB2288.summary: An act to amend Sections 65658.1 and 65658.5 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 22.

analysis.notes: Forces cities to allow residential conversions in industrial zones, preempting employment land protections; industrial zones lack residential utility infrastructure and cities cannot require developers to fund water, sewer, pedestrian, or parks upgrades needed for residential occupancy.
analysis.model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Claude Code) — D criterion reanalysis
analysis.comms_brief:
AB2288 forces cities to allow residential conversions in industrial-zoned areas, directly preempting employment land protections that communities have established through local planning.
• Preempts industrial land use designations: cities lose authority over employment zone preservation, undermining economic development strategies and tax base
• Threatens jobs and economic development: converting industrial to residential removes land cities rely on for tax-generating employment — a one-way transition with no reversal
• Infrastructure mismatch: industrial zones lack residential utilities, parks, and pedestrian infrastructure — cities cannot require developers to fund the needed upgrades for livable residential use
• No local environmental review: conversions proceed ministerially even on sites with contamination history or incompatible adjacent operations
Recommended: CSF should strongly oppose; partner with economic development organizations and labor groups on employment land preservation advocacy.

AB2295.title: Regional housing need: affordable housing.
AB2295.author: Natasha Johnson
AB2295.summary: An act to add Section 65584.2.3 to the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 22.

analysis.notes: AB 2295 adds Section 65584.2.3 to the Government Code, which relates to the Regional Housing Need Allocation (RHNA) process with a focus on affordable housing. Bills modifying RHNA methodology or allocation formulas partially constrain how cities plan and zone by adjusting the state-imposed housing quotas they must accommodate, representing a moderate local control override and moderate mandate pressure. The bill's early stage and limited digest prevent precise scoring of infrastructure cost impacts, though RHNA-driven compliance obligations routinely generate indirect staffing and planning burdens for localities.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2295 modifies the Regional Housing Need Allocation process to adjust how affordable housing need is calculated and assigned to cities and counties, potentially increasing or reshaping local housing quotas outside of local deliberation.
• RHNA changes constrain local General Plan and zoning choices by setting state-imposed numerical targets cities must plan for, regardless of local infrastructure, capacity, or community preference.
• Revised allocation formulas may increase the share of affordable units cities are required to site, effectively directing land use policy from Sacramento rather than city hall.
• Cities that fail to meet adjusted RHNA targets face builder's remedy exposure and loss of local zoning authority, creating strong compliance pressure even if penalties are not explicit in this bill.
Recommended: Monitor — bill is pre-committee with limited text available; risk level depends heavily on whether the allocation formula increases local quotas or imposes new methodology; full bill text should be reviewed once available.

analysis.notes: AB 2342 amends Government Code §65912.157, which governs transit-oriented development (TOD) streamlining under existing law. Amendments to this section typically extend or expand by-right or ministerial approval pathways for housing near transit, limiting local zoning discretion and conditioning or restricting design review and conditional use processes for qualifying projects. The infrastructure burden is indirect — increased density near transit stations pressures utility and traffic capacity without explicit fee restriction language confirmed in the text.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2342 amends the state's transit-oriented development statute (Gov. Code §65912.157) to alter streamlining provisions that override local zoning and reduce discretionary review for housing projects near transit.
• Amendments to §65912.157 typically expand or extend ministerial/by-right approval pathways, stripping cities of project-level discretion over qualifying TOD housing proposals.
• Existing TOD streamlining law limits the objective standards and conditions cities may impose, constraining local design review and conditional use permit authority.
• Any expansion of project eligibility or approval mandates forces cities to approve density or housing types beyond what local planning processes would otherwise allow.
• Increased development density near transit stations creates indirect infrastructure capacity pressure without confirmed local mitigation authority.
Recommended: Monitor — bill is newly introduced with no committee analysis or amendments available; full text review needed to confirm scope of changes to ministerial approval thresholds and eligibility criteria.

analysis.notes: AB 2433 amends Government Code §65915 (the Density Bonus Law), which already constitutes a state preemption of local zoning by requiring cities to grant density bonuses and reduced development standards to qualifying projects. Any amendment expanding eligibility, increasing bonus levels, or broadening concession/incentive rights would further erode local discretion over height, setbacks, parking, and unit counts. The bill's full text is needed to confirm the specific mechanism, but density bonus amendments structurally limit conditions cities may impose and compel approvals beyond locally preferred densities.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2433 amends California's Density Bonus Law (Gov. Code §65915), likely expanding the bonus levels, eligible project types, or required concessions/incentives that cities must grant to qualifying housing projects, further constraining local zoning authority.
• Local Control Override: Density Bonus Law amendments add to an existing state preemption framework, reducing cities' ability to apply local zoning standards — including height limits, setbacks, and unit counts — to qualifying projects.
• Removes Discretionary Review: Density bonus projects already limit the conditions cities may impose; an expansion of eligible projects or concession rights further narrows the scope of permissible local design and land use review.
• Mandates Development: Cities are legally required to grant density bonuses and any mandated concessions/incentives to qualifying applicants — any broadening of eligibility or bonus tiers increases the volume of projects cities cannot decline or substantially condition.
Recommended: Monitor — full bill text should be reviewed to confirm scope of amendments before determining whether to oppose; if bonus tiers or concession rights are significantly expanded, escalate to Oppose.

analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2480 amends California's Density Bonus Law (Gov. Code §65915) to require cities to grant additional density to qualifying student housing developments, overriding local zoning and limiting discretionary review for those projects.
• Partial state preemption of local zoning: cities must approve density bonuses for student housing projects beyond what local plans would otherwise allow, regardless of neighborhood context or infrastructure readiness.
• Restricted discretionary review: density bonus entitlements under §65915 significantly curtail the conditions, design standards, and hearing processes cities may apply to qualifying projects, reducing meaningful local input.
• Effective mandate on development type and density: by requiring cities to grant bonuses for a specific housing typology (student housing), the bill steers local land use decisions toward state-preferred outcomes rather than community-determined planning priorities.
Recommended: Monitor/Oppose — risk is significant but scoped to student housing projects; cities with university-adjacent neighborhoods face the greatest exposure to overridden zoning and compressed review authority.

analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2601 expands and entrenches multiple ministerial, by-right housing approval pathways — covering urban lot splits, ADUs, SB 35-style streamlining, and subdivision maps — stripping cities of the discretionary review, conditions, and CEQA tools they use to shape development to fit local needs.

• Prevents cities from applying locally-tailored objective standards or infrastructure conditions to projects approved under these streamlined tracks
• Unfunded compliance burden — cities must administer expanded ministerial review tracks with no state staffing or funding offset
Recommended: Oppose — the bill compounds existing state preemptions across multiple statutes, accelerating the erosion of local planning authority with no accountability mechanism for communities that bear the consequences

analysis.notes: AB 2702 is titled as a streamlined approval bill for residential care facilities for the elderly (RCFEs), a land use category that cities currently regulate through discretionary zoning and permitting. analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2702 would establish a streamlined (likely ministerial, by-right) approval pathway for residential care facilities for the elderly, overriding local discretionary zoning and permitting authority for this use class.
• Preempts local zoning by mandating approval of RCFEs regardless of whether the use is permitted or conditionally permitted under local ordinance
• Eliminates discretionary review tools — design review, conditional use permits, public hearings — that cities use to evaluate neighborhood compatibility and site suitability for care facilities
• Likely triggers CEQA exemption for qualifying projects, removing a key environmental and community review tool
• May prevent cities from imposing infrastructure conditions (traffic, utility, access improvements) as a condition of approval
Recommended: Monitor pending full bill text and committee referral — if "streamlined" is confirmed as ministerial/by-right with CEQA exemption, escalate to Oppose based on removal of all local discretion over a sensitive land use.

SB1258.title: Streamlined housing approvals: hazardous waste sites.
SB1258.author: Scott Wiener
SB1258.summary: An act to amend Section 65913.4 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Introduced. Read first time. To Com. on RLS. for assignment. To print.
From printer. May be acted upon on or after March 22.

analysis.notes: SB 1258 amends Government Code §65913.4 — the SB 35 streamlined ministerial approval statute — to expand its applicability to hazardous waste sites, which are currently excluded. This directly preempts local zoning and discretionary review authority by mandating ministerial (by-right) approval for qualifying multifamily projects on sites that cities may have deliberately excluded from residential development pipelines due to contamination concerns. The cost-to-cities risk is moderate: ministerial approval pathways prevent cities from conditioning approvals on full remediation timelines or site-specific infrastructure improvements, potentially shifting liability management and public health oversight costs onto local governments.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1258 expands the SB 35 ministerial housing approval mandate to hazardous waste sites, stripping cities of discretionary authority to condition or deny housing projects on contaminated land.
• Eliminates local discretionary review — including public hearings, design review, and conditional use permits — for qualifying projects on hazardous waste sites via ministerial approval mandate
• Preempts cities' ability to apply site-specific remediation conditions or sequencing requirements before housing approvals on contaminated parcels
• Removes cities' authority to pace or stage approvals in coordination with state and federal environmental cleanup timelines, potentially forcing approvals before remediation is complete
• Limits cities' ability to impose infrastructure and public health conditions tied to contamination status, shifting risk management responsibility onto local governments without state funding backstop
Recommended: Oppose — mandating ministerial approval on hazardous waste sites removes the last layer of local oversight protecting residents from premature development on contaminated land, with no mechanism to ensure remediation is complete before occupancy.

analysis.notes: SB 1353 amends Health & Safety Code §11834.23, which governs how cities and counties may treat alcohol/drug recovery and treatment facilities in residential zones. Existing law generally requires these facilities (6 or fewer residents) to be treated as a residential use by right — this bill likely modifies the conditions or scope of that mandate, potentially expanding the categories of facilities entitled to residential use classification or limiting the local conditions cities may impose. The local control risk stems from the state overriding local zoning discretion to compel residential treatment of these facilities and limiting what local governments may require before granting that status.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1353 amends state law governing when alcohol and drug recovery or treatment facilities must be treated as a residential use of property, potentially expanding state preemption of local zoning and limiting local conditions on these facilities.
• Existing §11834.23 already requires cities to classify qualifying facilities as residential by right — amendments could broaden the facilities entitled to this override, reducing local discretion over what facilities operate in residential neighborhoods.
• By mandating residential use classification, the bill limits cities' ability to apply local zoning standards, neighborhood compatibility review, or conditions tailored to community context.
• Any expansion of "by right" residential status further erodes the ability of local governments to evaluate siting, density of similar uses, or proximity concerns through discretionary review.
Recommended: Monitor — the full bill text is not available and the specific amendment language is critical to determining whether this materially expands state preemption beyond current law; fetch full text before taking a formal position.

analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1383 expands California's Density Bonus Law by conditioning or restructuring incentives and concessions tied to labor standards, further restricting what conditions cities may impose on qualifying density bonus projects.
• Density Bonus Law projects already limit local discretion; expanding incentive/concession provisions narrows the conditions cities can lawfully require of developers, reducing negotiating leverage.
• Cities cannot apply locally preferred design, community, or infrastructure standards to projects that qualify for and invoke expanded concessions under §65915.
• Broader use of density bonus concessions effectively mandates approval of higher-density projects on terms cities have not chosen, overriding locally adopted plans.
Recommended: Monitor — the full text should be reviewed to confirm whether labor standard conditions expand or further entrench concession mandates that restrict local authority; amendments may materially affect the risk profile.

analysis.notes: AB 748 requires cities and counties to establish a preapproved plan program for single-family and multifamily housing units, meaning qualifying projects that use state- or locally-preapproved plans must receive streamlined (likely ministerial) approval — reducing or eliminating discretionary design review for conforming projects. This partially overrides local zoning and design authority by mandating acceptance of plan templates the locality may not have chosen, and creates staffing and administrative costs to stand up and administer the program without guaranteed state funding.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 748 requires local governments to create and administer a preapproved housing plan program, compelling ministerial or streamlined approval of projects using those plans and curtailing local discretionary design review.
• Partial preemption of local zoning authority: cities must accept preapproved plan templates and cannot apply full discretionary design or conditional-use review to qualifying projects.
• Discretionary review curtailed: by definition, "preapproved" plans bypass or compress the design review and public hearing processes cities currently use to ensure projects fit neighborhood context and local standards.
• Mandated program administration: cities must build and maintain a preapproved plan library and intake process — an unfunded compliance burden that shifts staffing costs to existing residents and local budgets.
• Indirect pressure toward higher-density approvals: once plans are preapproved, applicants have a streamlined pathway that effectively overrides local preference on density and form.
Recommended: Oppose unless amended — bill lacks (1) explicit local option to set objective design standards for preapproved templates, (2) any state funding offset for program administration costs, and (3) clarity that cities retain authority to deny projects on infrastructure-capacity grounds.

analysis.notes: SB 908 adds Government Code §65850.73, which would preempt local building and zoning codes that impose requirements stricter than the California Building Code for residential window replacements — directly limiting cities' authority to condition window retrofit permits on local design, energy, or historic standards. It also adds Civil Code §4754, which would restrict HOA and local architectural controls over window replacement projects, further eroding discretionary review at the local level.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 908 preempts local building codes and discretionary review standards by barring cities from imposing any residential window replacement requirements beyond state California Building Code minimums.
• Criterion A — Cities lose authority to enforce locally-adopted window standards (energy performance, design compatibility, historic district requirements) that exceed state CBC minimums, a direct zoning and land use preemption.
• Criterion B — Removes cities' discretionary permit review for window replacement projects by mandating ministerial compliance with state standards only, stripping design review and conditional approval authority.
• Criterion D — Cities bear unfunded compliance and enforcement burden to update local codes and permitting workflows to conform to the new state floor, with no offsetting state funding provided.
Recommended: Oppose — bill explicitly strips local discretionary review and preempts locally-adopted building standards with no mechanism for cities to maintain community-specific requirements.

analysis.notes: AB 1621 amends both the Housing Accountability Act (HAA, §65589.5) and the postentitlement phase permit statute (§65913.3), which together restrict cities' ability to deny or condition entitled housing projects and require ministerial processing of postentitlement permits (building, grading, encroachment, etc.). Extending or tightening HAA provisions further limits cities' grounds to reject or modify compliant projects, while strengthening §65913.3 removes remaining local discretion over the permit-issuance phase of approved housing developments.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 1621 amends the Housing Accountability Act and postentitlement phase permit law to further constrain cities' authority to deny, condition, or delay housing projects that have already received entitlements.
• Strengthened HAA provisions narrow the grounds on which cities may lawfully disapprove or reduce the density of a compliant housing project, expanding state override of local land use decisions.
• Tightened postentitlement permit rules (§65913.3) push building, grading, and encroachment permits toward ministerial approval, removing remaining city discretion to impose conditions or require corrections during the permit phase.
• Cities that fail to comply face heightened HAA liability — including attorney's fees and court-ordered approvals — creating significant legal and financial exposure that effectively coerces approval of projects cities might otherwise condition.
• Combined amendments compress the window in which local governments can raise infrastructure, design, or public-safety concerns after entitlement is granted.
Recommended: Oppose — dual amendments to HAA and postentitlement law stack local control restrictions with no offsetting mechanism for cities to address infrastructure or public-safety concerns post-entitlement.

analysis.notes: SB 1361 amends Government Code §65912.157, which sits within California's Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) streamlining framework. This section governs ministerial by-right approvals for housing near transit — meaning cities are likely required to approve qualifying projects without discretionary review, overriding local zoning, design review, and CEQA processes. The infrastructure burden is moderate because ministerial approvals near transit stations can prevent cities from conditioning projects on capacity improvements for water, sewer, or traffic, shifting costs to existing residents.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1361 amends the state's transit-oriented development streamlining statute (Gov. Code §65912.157) to expand or adjust mandatory ministerial approvals for housing near transit, overriding local zoning and review authority in affected corridors.
• Ministerial by-right approval language in §65912.157 removes city discretion over qualifying TOD projects, eliminating design review, public hearings, and conditional use permits regardless of local planning priorities.
• The bill preempts local zoning standards near transit stations, forcing cities to permit housing densities and types that may conflict with adopted general plans and community-driven land use decisions.
• Ministerial approvals block cities from conditioning project approvals on infrastructure improvements, potentially requiring existing residents to absorb capacity costs for water, sewer, and traffic generated by new development.
• As an amendment to existing TOD streamlining law, the bill may expand the geographic scope or eligible project types, compounding local control loss across a wider range of transit corridors.
Recommended: Oppose — ministerial approval mandates strip cities of all discretionary tools and infrastructure conditioning authority, with no replacement mechanism for lost local planning control.

analysis.notes: AB 2296 amends the RHNA methodology and allocation process under Government Code §§65584.03–65584.05, which governs how regional councils of government distribute housing need quotas to member jurisdictions. Modifications to the allocation methodology or appeal process can effectively increase housing numbers assigned to individual cities, overriding local preferences on growth scale (Criterion A/C). There is no direct fee restriction or removal of discretionary review, but compliance with higher RHNA allocations creates indirect staffing, rezoning, and infrastructure pressure on cities (Criterion D).
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2296 amends the RHNA allocation methodology and process, reshaping how housing quotas are assigned to cities and counties across California.
• Modifications to §65584.04 allocation criteria can increase housing numbers assigned to individual jurisdictions beyond what local general plans and infrastructure capacity anticipate, effectively overriding local growth decisions.
• Changes to the allocation appeal process under §65584.05 may narrow or constrain cities' ability to challenge inappropriate quotas, reducing a key procedural safeguard for local governments.
• Higher or less-contestable RHNA numbers trigger mandatory rezoning obligations and expose cities to builder's remedy risk if Housing Elements fall out of compliance, compounding the loss of local discretion.
• Compliance with revised allocations imposes unfunded staffing, rezoning, and environmental review burdens on city planning departments without dedicated state funding.
Recommended: Monitor — the bill passed the Assembly 76-0, suggesting broad political consensus; risk depends heavily on the specific allocation formula amendments, and Senate committee amendments could sharpen or soften impact. Request full bill text to confirm scope before escalating to Oppose.

analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2415 amends the state's transit-oriented development statute to create an "alternative plans" pathway that overrides local zoning and entitlement authority for qualifying housing projects near transit.
• Partially preempts local zoning — cities must process TOD projects under a state-defined alternative plan framework, limiting their ability to apply community-specific development standards
• Restricts discretionary review — by-right or streamlined TOD approvals reduce cities' authority to impose design, density, and compatibility conditions through public hearings or conditional use processes
• Effectively mandates approval of housing types and densities beyond local preference — the alternative plans mechanism is a state-imposed pathway cities cannot easily opt out of for qualifying projects
• Indirect infrastructure risk — reduced conditioning authority on ministerial approvals may limit cities' ability to require developer-funded infrastructure improvements near transit corridors
Recommended: Oppose unless amended — the bill layers an additional state override onto local TOD planning without clear replacement authority for cities to address infrastructure or community compatibility concerns

analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1367 adds Government Code §65850.05 to preempt local zoning authority over detention facilities, stripping cities and counties of their ability to determine where — and under what conditions — such facilities may be sited within their jurisdictions.
• Explicit state preemption of local zoning: cities cannot apply their own land use standards to block or restrict detention facility siting, removing a core function of local planning authority.
• Forced approval mandate: the bill effectively compels local governments to approve qualifying detention facility projects regardless of community land use preferences, functioning as a builder's remedy for this facility type.
• Infrastructure condition limitations: ministerial or restricted approval pathways prevent cities from imposing infrastructure improvement conditions, shifting capacity costs (traffic, utilities, public safety) to existing residents.
• Loss of discretionary review: cities lose the ability to subject these projects to conditional use permits, design review, or public hearings that would otherwise protect neighborhood character and infrastructure adequacy.
Recommended: Oppose — bill eliminates local zoning authority over a sensitive land use category with no replacement mechanism for infrastructure cost recovery or community input.
